text: The surgeon uses electrocautery to address adhesions in the right lower quadrant, dealing with omentum and small bowel adhesions as they progress towards the midline. The retro-rectus mesh is visible, and the previously placed Versatex mesh appears fractured at the site of hernia recurrence. A small thermal injury to the serosa is noted and repaired with 3-0 silk sutures.

The surgeon gently pulls the omentum and bowel away from the mesh, revealing fine adhesive tissue fibers that allow for dissection approximately one centimeter from the edge of the omentum or bowel. A metallic tack from a previous surgery is visible. The dissection continues in the left upper quadrant beneath the left costal margin to create more space.

The surgeon dissects the area where the new mesh will be placed, focusing on the midline of the abdomen and moving towards the patient's left colostomy site. The turquoise mesh is visible to the left of the screen. The hernia is identified, and the surgeon carefully dissects the incarcerated bowel, noting the sharp edge of the mesh around the defect, which suggests mesh fracture due to tension between closures.

The surgeon encounters thickened peritoneum at the edges of the hernia, which initially poses a challenge for dissection due to the risk of bowel injury. By spreading the tissue further, a clear plane is revealed, facilitating safer dissection to free the bowel. A proline suture from a previous operation is visible within the hernia.

The surgeon has successfully reduced all the bowel and is now creating adequate space for mesh overlap. The hernia measures approximately eight by six centimeters, and a 25 by 20 Symbotex mesh is chosen for the IPOM repair. The mobilized left colon is visible extending down to the pelvis, and the surgeon continues to dissect behind the colon to ensure the mesh sits flat. A metallic tack is being removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon begins closing the hernia defect using permanent sutures, starting from the far side and imbricating the hernia sac and pseudosac. Another suture is placed from the near side to bring the edges together, with reduced pressure set to about eight for the closure.

The surgeon positions a 25 by 20 Symbotex mesh, using absorbable tackers at cardinal locations to orient and secure it. The mesh is carefully tucked behind the colon, avoiding areas with nerves, particularly over the psoas in the inguinal region. A V-lock suture is run around the perimeter of the mesh, and a sponge is used to manage minor bleeding in the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon carefully places sutures in the left lower quadrant, avoiding the retroperitoneal area to prevent nerve injury, and positions the mesh behind the left colon. The suturing continues around the perimeter of the mesh, ensuring secure placement.
